data.title: Man plunges to ground after blast at Hong Kong flat
data.description:
A man has been blown out of a residential building after an explosion at a flat in Hong Kong.
Police said the man plunged to the ground after a blast was heard at a building at 6.40pm on Monday at 43 Pak Tai Street in Kowloon City.
He remained conscious and was sent to Queen Elizabeth Hospital in Yau Ma Tei. The authorities are responding to the situation.
The seven-storey building at 43 Pak Tai Street was built in 1960 and has three flats on each floor.
More to follow …

data.title: How good mobility can keep you healthy after 30, and the best exercises to help you
data.description:
As people age, it is not uncommon for many to let out a muted groan when getting out of bed in the morning.
But if you “oof” every time you get in a car or “aargh” while bending over to pick up something, it may be time to prioritise exercises that target your mobility.
Dr Miho Tanaka, a sports medicine surgeon at Massachusetts General Hospital and Harvard Medical School in the US, said good mobility is recognised as an important part of overall health. She pointed to research showing that high mobility has long-term effects in reducing rates of cancer, cardiovascular disease, dementia and depression.
“It’s not just about how you look and how your level of fitness is at any given point,” Tanaka said. “For anyone who wants to stay active later in life, there has to be a deliberate process of maintaining that level of activity by doing mobility exercises.”
Mobility equals strength plus flexibility
Mobility and flexibility are often confused, said Jessica Valant, a physical therapist and Pilates instructor near Denver, in the US state of Colorado.
Flexibility is simply the extent to which a muscle can lengthen, whereas mobility involves using muscle strength to interact with a network of joints, tendons, ligaments and synovial fluid, which lubricates and nourishes joints.

That network around the shoulders, spine, hips, pelvis and knees needs to work well together, otherwise it becomes difficult to reach things in high cabinets, squat down to tie your shoes or lift your grandchildren, let alone take part in outdoor activities or sports.
Mobility exercises strengthen your muscles while increasing your range of motion, which allows you to be more functional with age, Valant said.
“Motion is lotion, which is a saying we use in physical therapy all the time,” she said.
Two early warning signs
Starting from about 30 years old, muscles and tendons start to lose collagen, Tanaka said. That is the same substance that in hair and skin relates to going grey and getting wrinkles.
Without regular exercise, weaker muscles put extra pressure on joints, she said. Even active people lose muscle in areas that are overlooked at the gym, such as the rotator cuff around the shoulder and postural muscles that support the spine and neck.

Tanaka said two early warning signs likely point to the need for mobility training.
The first is aching joints and muscle stiffness without first exerting a lot of effort. Back soreness after a long day of sitting at a desk could signal limited hip or pelvic mobility, she said. Aching knees after being on your feet all day may be a sign of weak quadriceps.
The second sign is more acute pain, such as when you do not get injured but you still experience swollen or painful joints for several days after taking part in a sport like basketball or skiing for the first time in a while.
She cautioned against following a recent social media challenge to test mobility by standing up from a seated position on the floor without using your hands.
“I’ve seen some injuries from that, so I definitely don’t recommend that,” she said.


Instead, try sitting in a chair and standing up without putting your hands on the chair arms. Next, try to stand on one foot. If you can do both, that is a good sign.
Tai chi and yoga are also both great for mobility training, she said.
Other recommended exercises
Dr Corey Simon, associate professor of orthopaedic surgery at Duke University School of Medicine, urged people to prioritise mobility before they experience pain in their joints or difficulty moving around.
“You don’t have to have a gym to do this,” Simon said.

He suggested working body weight exercises into your day. That could include setting an hourly timer to get up and walk around the house. When you return to your desk, add chair sits to work the quads, hamstrings, calves and glutes. Assuming your balance is OK, doing push-ups against a wall or a countertop helps to mobilise the shoulders.
Once those exercises become easy, add exercise bands to build strength.
Valant said that people who are sedentary should simply start walking every day.
“It’s the lowest barrier to entry,” she said.
Beyond that, she suggests adding 10 minutes of mobility training to whatever exercise you are doing. She shows short mobility routines on her YouTube channel targeting the hips, spine and full body.
Some basic hip movements include lying on your side and lifting your top leg; lying on your back and squeezing your knees to your chest; and sitting up with your feet together, lowering your knees towards the floor.
For the spine, try the cat-cow stretch, arching and curving your back with hands and knees on the floor. Or sit in a chair with feet on the floor, twisting your shoulders to both sides.
“This isn’t something that changes in five days, but I tell people, give it four weeks,” she said. “You will see results over time.”

data.title: China prioritises export controls, supply-chain security to shield economy in 2026
data.description:
China has made strengthening export control rules and safeguarding supply-chain resilience top priorities this year, as officials move to secure the nation’s economic interests amid rising geopolitical friction.
The Ministry of Commerce said it would enhance legal frameworks and risk prevention and tighten the “safety net for opening up” as one of eight core work areas, according to a statement released on Sunday after its annual work conference in Beijing.
China has increasingly used export controls to protect national security and critical technologies amid trade, supply-chain and diplomatic disputes.
On Thursday, the ministry announced a review of Meta Platforms’ US$2.5 billion acquisition of Manus, which developed its products in China, to determine whether the deal complies with export control and technology transfer rules.
The Chinese-founded artificial intelligence (AI) agent start-up relocated to Singapore – and reportedly laid off most of its Beijing staff – last year, in a move widely seen as a bid to access more overseas funding and clients.
As China’s race with the US for AI supremacy accelerates, analysts have said the review signalled a tightening of oversight on foreign stakes in sensitive technologies, even as founders move operations abroad to sidestep geopolitical scrutiny.
Beijing also recently announced a ban on dual-use exports – goods with both commercial and military applications, such as drones and rare earth elements – to military end users in Japan amid a protracted diplomatic row with Tokyo.
Introduced on January 6, the restrictions are expected to be a focal point in Washington on Monday, when Japanese officials meet with finance ministers from other Group of Seven (G7) nations to outline Tokyo’s stance on the curbs.




At the meeting, the United States will also press its G7 partners to step up efforts to reduce reliance on critical minerals from China, Reuters previously reported.
Facing mounting external pressures, Beijing is increasingly turning to domestic growth drivers while diversifying investment channels to support the economy.
At the work conference, the commerce ministry said it would focus on boosting domestic demand, expanding opening up and widening the scope for two-way investment over the next five years.
Officials also pledged to spur service consumption, improve trade-in programmes for consumer goods and develop the digital, green and healthcare markets.
They added that Beijing would enhance free-trade zones such as the Hainan Free Trade Port, and called for measures to attract more foreign investment – particularly in the service sector – while encouraging domestic firms to expand overseas.
“[We will] effectively manage outbound investment, guiding a rational and orderly cross-border layout of industrial supply chains,” the ministry said, pledging to deepen economic and trade cooperation under the Belt and Road Initiative.
The push aligns with a rise in outbound direct investment (ODI) as companies diversify revenue streams – particularly in emerging markets – to offset weak domestic demand and hedge against trade uncertainty.
China’s non-financial ODI to countries taking part in the initiative rose 23.7 per cent year on year to US$30 billion in the first three quarters of 2025, accounting for more than a quarter of the total, according to a report by consulting firm EY.

data.title: Surging memory-chip prices force Chinese smartphone maker Meizu to abandon launch
data.description:
The global memory-chip crunch has forced a mainland Chinese smartphone manufacturer to pull the plug on the launch of a new handset, making it one of the first cancellations affecting the wider industry due to surging prices of the key component.
DreamSmart Group said that its Meizu smartphone brand had cancelled the release of Meizu 22 Air, a slim handset similar to Apple’s iPhone Air.
“The sharp rise in memory prices since the fourth quarter has not only impacted [production] costs, but also dealt a huge shock to our commercial plan for smartphones,” Wan Zhiqiang, the chief marketing officer at DreamSmart, said in a live-streamed event on Saturday.
The entire industry was downsizing its product portfolios amid a supply chain crunch, Wan said, adding that Meizu would focus its resources on products that the company truly needed.

Counterpoint Research said last week that memory-chip prices had jumped 40 to 50 per cent in the fourth quarter of 2025. It added that prices could rise by another 40 to 50 per cent in this year’s first quarter and another 20 per cent in the second quarter.
Meizu, founded in 2007, was once one of the most popular smartphone brands on the mainland, but it gradually faded due to the cutthroat domestic competition.
It was acquired in 2022 by carmaker Zhejiang Geely Holding Group. In 2024, Meizu announced that it would terminate new projects related to traditional smartphones to pursue an “all in AI” strategy.
The cancellation of Meizu 22 Air’s launch reflects the broader problems in the global memory supply chain, as leading manufacturers prioritise capacity for customers tied to the surging demand for artificial intelligence chips.
The soaring memory prices have also sent shock waves through the global electronics industry, with Chinese manufacturers like Xiaomi and Honor announcing plans to raise the prices of their smartphones and tablets.
Xiaomi president Lu Weibing said in an earnings call in November that Xiaomi planned to offset the situation by optimising its product mix and increasing the average selling price of its products.
“[Some players] will fall behind or even leave the game,” Lu said. “In trying times, companies will have to compete on which one is better prepared or more deep-pocketed.”

data.title: Official accused of abusing Chinese athlete Wang Li sacked, claims found ‘basically true’
data.description:
An official accused of verbally and physically abusing China’s triple Asian Games dragon boat gold medallist Wang Li has been dismissed, according to the provincial sports body that oversees her training.
Wang, who won three gold medals at the Hangzhou Games held in 2023, had accused her boss Fan Jiwen, the director of the Songmao Sports Training Base, of abusing her for months after she refused to hand over the prize money she received from the national team last December.
This weekend, the Yunnan Provincial Sports Bureau announced that an investigation had found Wang’s allegations “basically true”, and added that further inquiries were under way.
The 23-year-old Wang and her teammates won gold in the women’s 200m, 500m and 1,000m races at Hangzhou, making her the first Yunnan athlete to claim three Asian Games golds in the discipline.
On December 11, Wang took to the Chinese social media platform RedNote to report that after returning to the Yunnan Songmao base, Fan demanded the 150,000 yuan (US$21,500) bonus she had received from the national team.

She claimed she faced continual “verbal abuse and corporal punishment”, and that Fan told sports authorities she was “retiring due to injury”, effectively ending her career.
The provincial sports bureau launched an investigation several days later.
As the probe got under way, Wang posted another video on December 16, sharing an audio recording from August 2024 that she said revealed the kind of pressure she was facing.
In the recording, base staff told her that several senior officials, including the deputy director, were aware of the situation and intended to talk to her about retirement. They advised her to retire voluntarily, saying that if the matter escalated, “they can give you an abnormal retirement from the team because you do not follow the organisation’s arrangements”.
Wang also said police contacted her after base coaches reported her missing, asking about her whereabouts, a situation she described as distressing and disruptive.
Twenty-five days after exposing the matter, Wang resurfaced on social media last Friday, saying she was safe and staying at a friend’s home.

“I met a joint investigative team from the Yunnan sports bureau on December 25, and presented evidence,” Wang said in the latest video.
The sports bureau confirmed in a statement the following day that Wang’s reports were “basically true” and that Fan had been dismissed. It also said relevant authorities had opened a formal case against him.
Wang did not clarify whether she would continue competing or retire, but the official statement indicated she remained an athlete for now.
“The Yunnan Provincial Sports Bureau will ensure appropriate protection and support for athlete Wang in accordance with relevant athlete management policies,” the statement said.
“We have launched a special rectification campaign to strengthen discipline, improve team management and better safeguard athletes’ rights and welfare,” the bureau added.

data.title: Record-setting marathon attracts 1 in 4 runners from outside Hong Kong
data.description:
About a quarter of the runners for the coming Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon are from outside the city, a record for the annual race, the organiser has said.
Authorities also warned that road closures for Sunday’s marathon will affect services along 269 bus and 40 minibus routes, and called on residents to plan their trips in advance.
The Hong Kong, China Association of Athletics Affiliates, which organises the event, said on Monday that among the 74,000 people set to take part this year, about 25 per cent would be non-locals - a new record.
Such runners had come from around 110 countries and regions, it added.
“The Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon is an internationally acclaimed event, capturing a significant place in the global long-haul running community, and therefore many international runners have wished to come to Hong Kong to participate in the mega-event,” association executive director Dennis Ng Yu-ho said.
Last year, about 20 per cent of the runners, or 15,000, were non-locals.
The organiser had earlier vowed to enhance overseas promotion of this year’s event in a bid to attract more non-local runners.
The overall number of participants this year is also comparable with that of the 2025 event.
In October, the association said more than 120,000 people had signed up for a ballot for the quotas this year, representing a record high.
The 2025 event also saw the disqualification of four runners, who were found to be wearing other people’s bibs when they crossed the finish line.
Ng said on Monday that the incidents were caused by the runners’ manager mixing up the bibs, instead of any intentional swapping.
“We call on runners to verify information on their bibs, and if they find out the bibs do not belong to them, they should reach out to ask as soon as possible for rectification,” he said.

Sunday’s event will consist of a full marathon, a half marathon, a 10km (6.2-mile) run and a 10km wheelchair race.
The race for wheelchair users will be the first batch to start, leaving Wan Chai Sports Ground at 5.10am.
After that, competitors for the 10km and half-marathon will go next, with the first batches of runners heading off at 5.30am from the Island Eastern Corridor and Tsim Sha Tsui, respectively.
The first batch of marathon runners will depart at 6am from Tsim Sha Tsui.
All races will finish at Victoria Park in Causeway Bay.
In response to the race, some major roads on both sides of the Victoria Harbour, including Nathan Road, Central Kowloon Bypass, Island Eastern Corridor, the western and eastern harbour crossings, will be closed or partially shut down in phases.
As a result of the road closures, services along 269 bus routes – 220 daytime ones and 49 overnight ones – will be affected.
The event will also affect 40 minibus routes, comprising 34 daytime and six overnight ones.
Services along eight MTR lines will also start earlier on Sunday to allow runners to reach their starting points, while bus operators have arranged 28 special routes for participants.
The first trains for the Tuen Ma line and the Admiralty-bound East Rail line will depart at 3.25am.
“Overall, due to the influence of large-scale road closures, the road traffic and public transport services across Hong Kong Island, Kowloon and the New Territories will be subject to significant changes, and we also anticipate more congested traffic in main junctions than an average Sunday,” said Ann Wong On-yu, a senior transport officer of the Transport Department.
Wong called on commuters to plan their trips ahead and make the best of public transport on that day, especially the railway services.
Drivers could get more information from the department’s website and the “HKeMobility” mobile app, she added.

data.title: Japan’s ‘genius’ chimpanzee Ai, who mastered Chinese characters, dies at 49
data.description:
Ai, a “genius” chimpanzee who could recognise more than 100 Chinese characters and the English alphabet, has died aged 49, Japanese researchers said.
Ai, which means love in Japanese, took part in studies on perception, learning and memory that advanced our understanding of primate intelligence, the Centre for the Evolutionary Origins of Human Behaviour at Kyoto University said in a statement.
She died on Friday from multiple organ failure and ailments related to old age, the school said.
Aside from mastering Chinese characters and the alphabet, Ai could also identify the Arabic numerals from zero to nine and 11 colours, primatologist Tetsuro Matsuzawa said in 2014.

In one study, Ai was presented with a computer screen displaying the Chinese character for pink, along with a pink square and an alternative purple square. The chimpanzee correctly chose the pink square, Matsuzawa said.
When shown an apple, Ai picked out a rectangle, a circle and a dot on the computer screen to draw a “virtual apple”, he said.
Her high ability made her the subject of a number of scholarly papers and media programmes, including studies published in the journal Nature, and earned her the nickname “genius” in popular media.
The chimpanzee from west Africa arrived at the university in 1977, and in 2000, gave birth to a son, Ayumu, whose abilities drew attention to studies of parent-child knowledge transfer, Japan’s Kyodo News said.

Ai’s studies helped to establish “an experimental framework for understanding the chimpanzee mind, providing a crucial foundation for considering the evolution of the human mind,” the Centre said.
“Ai was highly curious and actively participated in these studies, revealing various aspects of the chimpanzee mind for the first time.”

data.title: Gig workers’ strike in India spurs debate on human cost of delivery race
data.description:
A New Year’s Eve flash strike by gig workers in India who provide commerce connectivity, such as through 10-minute doorstep deliveries, has drawn attention to their welfare, including the risk of accidents amid physical exhaustion.
More than 200,000 gig workers staged protests across major Indian cities during the peak delivery period to highlight their demands for legislative protection, social security benefits and better wages, according to the Indian Federation of App Based Transport Workers.
They also protested against automated systems used by the platforms to penalise workers for late deliveries and reduce their ratings, provoking a debate over quick commerce, a trend that has caught on worldwide.
Quick commerce firms in India, such as Zomato, Blinkit and Swiggy have downplayed the recent strike, saying the disruptions only involved a fraction of workers, while simultaneously highlighting existing insurance and hourly daily earnings for workers’ welfare.

India’s gig economy is rapidly growing, with about 7.7 million workers in 2020-21, according to government think tank NITI Aayog. The workforce segment is projected to reach 23.5 million by 2029-30, making it a significant part of the economy.
A rapidly growing middle class has stoked demand for an array of near-instant delivery services that range from food from restaurants to groceries, providing employment to these workers.
But the emerging debate centres on sections of consumers questioning the service’s necessity if it pushes workers to jump traffic lights to meet schedules and maximise daily earnings.
Analysts note that the gig workers have cited valid concerns about their working conditions.
“Ultra-fast delivery models of 10-15 minutes materially change the risk and stress profile of gig work. Traffic and road safety is a real challenge for them in urban centres in India. Also there is a challenge with incentive design,” said Prabir Jha, founder and CEO of Prabir Jha People Advisory.
Issues with the data-tracking systems, penalties for delays and an opaque rating process added to the stress, while the social protection for such a workforce was “almost absent”, he added.
“When delivery commitments are framed as minutes rather than outcomes, risk is always transferred downwards, to riders who face pressure to rush, accept opaque incentive structures, and bear penalties for delays often outside their control,” said Raj Kapoor, president of India Blockchain Alliance.
“Who is accountable when safety is compromised? And how sustainable is a model that treats human labour as infinitely elastic?”
Quick commerce firms needed to shift from “speed absolutism to responsible service-level design”, Kapoor said.
He urged them to embrace transparent algorithms around penalties and incentives, include explicit safety buffers in workers’ delivery timelines, and determine minimum guaranteed earnings per shift instead of per delivery.

A fierce battle among India’s quick commerce firms to capture market share has turned the 10-minute delivery timeline into a gold standard, rather than explicit demand among consumers, observers say.
Blinkit leads in market share with about 40–45 per cent while others have been aggressively trying to match its speed to remain competitive.
Karan Taurani, executive vice-president at Elara Capital, a London-based investment bank, said there needed to be more clarity on welfare payouts for gig workers.
“I think the welfare payouts are restricted to a certain percentage of the gig workers, not to the entire community,” he said.
But he said 10-minute delivery models by themselves did not merit safety concerns as the firms had put in place measures such as having extensive networks of stores and robust technology to ensure deadlines could be comfortably met.
Coinciding with the unrest, the Ministry of Labour and Employment released draft rules under the Code on Social Security (Central) Rules, 2025 on December 31, proposing a framework for gig worker benefits including insurance and pensions.
Last year’s federal budget announced the formal registration of more than 10 million gig workers on an e-Shram portal and introduced initiatives to provide unique identity cards to gig workers to simplify access to welfare programmes, including health insurance.

Earlier this month, Zomato CEO Deepinder Goyal strongly defended the company’s delivery model against criticism of poor working conditions and 10-minute delivery pressures, suggesting the role was used as a flexible, part-time, or “stopgap” income source rather than a high-pressure, full-time job.
Observers have disputed the framing of gig work as “safe and sustainable” and a matter of choice.
“The facts are quite the contrary. Millions are dependent on gig work of this kind as their main source of income, and the practical realities they face remain deplorable,” said Aparna Mittal, a corporate lawyer and founder of Samana Centre, a leading anti-discrimination consultancy.
With gig workers forming a key part of the Indian workforce, Mittal said they needed more legal protection and urged for “all laws and regulations to be comprehensively looked at in totality”.
While there may be more incidents of worker unrest in the short term, analysts say the recent changes are likely to afford gig workers improved welfare and legal protection.
“There could be more strikes [by gig workers] but once there is clarity on payout … things will get more streamlined. There will be uncertainty over the three to six months but gig workers’ concerns are not clouding quick commerce in a big way,” Taurani said.
“I think quick commerce in India is here to stay.”

data.title: Hong Kong property: Citi lifts forecast after Morgan Stanley’s upgrade
data.description:
Major financial institutions, including Citi and Bank of America, are increasingly optimistic about Hong Kong’s residential property market this year, as lower mortgage rates and strong leasing demand change buying behaviour.
Citi raised its home price forecast to 8 per cent this year, up from 3 per cent in October, citing a faster-than-expected rebound last year.
“[There will be] further acceleration in 2027 under a multi-year upcycle,” said Griffin Chan, head of Asia-Pacific property research at Citi Research, on Friday. He added that home sales this year were likely to exceed new completions for the first time since 2019.
Chan said the revised forecast reflected a fundamental shift rather than speculative demand, noting that new land supply had fallen to a 14-year low, while available housing stock shrank by about 10 per cent last year.

Structural demand was also improving, Chan said, pointing to rising student numbers and continuing inflows of skilled workers, which were supporting leasing and future owner-occupier demand.
Citi’s upgrade follows a more bullish call from Morgan Stanley, which upgraded the city’s property sector to “attractive” on January 5 and forecast home prices to rise more than 10 per cent this year.
UBS, meanwhile, expects home prices to rise by around 5 per cent. The Swiss bank said falling mortgage rates were pushing tenants to buy, while some homeowners had withdrawn their homes from the resale market and shifted to leasing.
UBS said the sustained rise in rents over the past two years, even as prices fell, showed that buying demand had been deferred rather than destroyed.
However, UBS expected the rent momentum to moderate. “We have noticed that the growth rate of rents is slowing down due to rent-to-own conversions and the diminishing marginal effect of attracting professionals to Hong Kong,” said John Lam, the head of Asia property research at UBS.
Bank of America also expects a single-digit growth in residential prices this year.
The residential property market could see a stronger rebound in prices and transactions if the Federal Reserve’s policy easing path is more aggressive than expected, according to Ting Him Ho, Asia economist at Bank of America Merrill Lynch.
However, a “spillover from shocks in the equity market [could] largely tilt [the property market] to the downside in our view, given that valuations are already elevated”, he added.


Likewise, Bank of East Asia (BEA) expects the residential market recovery to continue, with prices set to rise between 6 and 8 per cent this year.
“Lower interest rates, strong rental demand and improving economic fundamentals will continue to solidify the recovery in the private residential market,” said Tristan Zhuo, chief economist at BEA.
OCBC is cautious about market activity despite forecasting a 5 per cent price increase this year.
Cindy Keung, an economist at OCBC Hong Kong, said that transaction volumes were little changed late last year, suggesting buyers remained selective even as prices recovered.
She added that recent price gains have begun to outpace rent growth, which could limit investment demand as yields peaked.
S&P Global Ratings said it expected demand to remain resilient, supported by rising rents and lower interest rates, but cautioned that abundant new supply and high inventory could hinder a meaningful rebound in prices
The rating agency said more than 26,000 completed but unsold units still remained on the market, with new supply this year broadly matching expected demand.
“We think Hong Kong’s home prices will likely remain rangebound in 2026,” said director Edward Chan.

data.title: Producers at Hong Kong toy fair bet on AI-powered plushies to double sales
data.description:
Dog and cat plushies powered by artificial intelligence (AI) could help double sales, according to manufacturers at Asia’s largest toy fair in Hong Kong, while some buyers have expressed concerns about using the technology in play items for children.
These hi-tech dolls were among the highlights at the 52nd edition of the Hong Kong Toys & Games Fair, which runs alongside several other trade shows until Thursday at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre in Wan Chai.
Vegas Yeung, owner of Aida Tech Limited, said on Monday AI stuffed toys in the shape of dogs or cats had been popular in mainland China in the past year. He was expecting business to double this year as he targeted relatively untapped overseas markets.
“My products used to sell for dozens of Hong Kong dollars and are now going for hundreds,” Yeung said, comparing traditional stuffed toys he produced with those equipped with AI capabilities.
He added that the market for “AI pets” on the mainland was more advanced than overseas, meaning the toys were a few generations ahead of those exported.
The stuffed AI cat would purr when touched, with movable feet, head and tail to interact with the user, which Yeung said tackled the human need for warmth, companionship and being understood, especially for the elderly who live alone.
The product could be used with different large language models from DeepSeek for the mainland market to ChatGPT for an overseas market, and would support all languages that these models support.
When asked about privacy concerns of having an AI product at home for overseas markets, Yeung said that his was no different from any other smart devices from fridges to lawnmowers that are connected to the Internet of Things and that he just needed to find the right customers.
“But we don’t really sell to the US market any more, the uncertainty makes it impossible to plan our business. We are now turning our efforts more to belt and road countries, as well as expanding our existing client bases in Europe, Asean, Japan and South Korea,” he said.
Guangdong Beiens Technology Company is also riding on the trend and produces devices with AI functions from printers to clocks that are useful to adults, to devices that help children learn mathematics and the alphabet.
Matt Hui, the firm’s overseas sales manager, said their products also support different large language models, but the costs would be different. He said they were less affected by the US tariffs last year as 90 per cent of their products were sold on the mainland.
“For parents, our products could be a way to introduce their children to AI in a more controlled environment, unlike the phone where it’s harder to control what children have access to on social media platforms,” he said.
